explanation: The requirement specifies that both prop_0 and prop_1 must always hold. In LTL, this is expressed using the global operator G. The conjunction operator (&) is used here so that both properties must be true at all times.
The system should always satisfy both prop_0 and prop_1, so globally both must hold.
The requirement is that both prop_0 and prop_1 hold globally. This can be expressed as a conjunction of globally prop_0 and globally prop_1, or equivalently, globally (prop_0 and prop_1).
The statement says the system shall satisfy both prop_0 and prop_1. In LTL, this is simply the conjunction of both atomic propositions, asserting that both must hold in the current state.
The requirement is that both prop_0 and prop_1 must hold (i.e., they are true). In LTL, since there is no temporal operator specified, this means that both must hold now (in the current state).

The requirement states that both prop_0 and prop_1 must hold globally throughout the execution. In LTL, this is expressed as the conjunction of 'globally prop_0' and 'globally prop_1', but we can combine this as 'globally (prop_0 and prop_1)'.

The requirement states that both prop_0 and prop_1 should hold. In LTL, this is represented as the conjunction of both atomic propositions: prop_0 & prop_1. This requirement is typically interpreted as holding in the initial state or at the current point.
The natural language requires that both prop_0 and prop_1 always hold. In LTL, this is expressed as globally (G) both properties hold.

The requirement states that both prop_0 and prop_1 must be true. Assuming this needs to hold globally throughout the system's execution, the LTL formula is G (prop_0 & prop_1), meaning that at all times, prop_0 and prop_1 hold.

The requirement is that both prop_0 and prop_1 should always be true, i.e., at all times in the execution of the system, both must hold simultaneously. This is represented in LTL using the globally (G) operator and the conjunction (&) of the two propositions.
